---
title: "Low Spikesedge (Cyperus hortensis)"
slug: "cyperus-hortensis"
group: "Plantae"
image: "https://inaturalist-open-data.s3.amazonaws.com/photos/354650780/medium.jpg"        # keep for thumbnails
image-alt: "Low Spikesedge"  # keep for a11y
tax_kingdom: "Plantae"
tax_class: "Liliopsida"
tax_order: "Poales"
tax_family: "Cyperaceae"
categories: ["Plantae", "Liliopsida", "Poales", "Cyperaceae"]   # these drive the chips + filtering
freeze: true
---

<figure class="species-figure">

<img src="https://inaturalist-open-data.s3.amazonaws.com/photos/354650780/medium.jpg" alt="Low Spikesedge" class="species-photo"/>

<figcaption class="photo-credit">

Photo: <a href="https://www.inaturalist.org/people/larvalfishguy">
larvalfishguy</a> (CC BY-NC) via <a href="https://www.inaturalist.org/observations/200956883">iNaturalist</a>

</figcaption>

</figure>

## Taxonomy

-   **Class:** Liliopsida
-   **Order:** Poales
-   **Family:** Cyperaceae

## Occurrence at DeLuca Preserve

-   [DeLuca Bio Blitz Species Observations](https://www.inaturalist.org/observations?project_id=191727&subview=map&taxon_id=779122)

## Conservation & rarity context

-   IUCN: Not Listed
-   **Rarity note:** Common species in Florida found in moist to wet habitats.

## Natural history notes

A small, grass-like sedge characterized by its slender stems and compact clusters of spikelets near the tips. In Florida, it is commonly found in moist to wet habitats such as ditches, marsh edges, and disturbed wetlands. It grows during the warmer months and often occurs as a subtle component of low, open vegetation.

## References / links

-   [iNaturalist species page](https://www.inaturalist.org/taxa/779122-Cyperus-hortensis)
-   [Local profile](https://florida.plantatlas.usf.edu/plant/species/2739)
